notable.primary_url_label: Historic Northampton: Samuel Stiles, the Gare Collection of Silvermaking and Watch Repair Tools

military_service.notes: Served in a company formed in Windsor CT during the War of 1812 with Britain. Stationed at Fort Trumbull (New London CT) in February 1813.

narrative_blocks.header: War of 1812 private, stationed Fort Trumbull
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Stiles served as a private in a company formed in Windsor CT during the War of 1812 with Britain, stationed at Fort Trumbull (New London) in February 1813. He was 50 years old at the time — old for a militiaman — but the Connecticut coast was being threatened by British naval blockade, and middle-aged men were drawn into local defense.

narrative_blocks.header: Died on his thirty-ninth wedding anniversary
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Stiles married Hannah Ellsworth in Windsor on October 15, 1787; he died at Windsor on October 15, 1826 — exactly thirty-nine years later, on their wedding anniversary. Hannah survived him by only fifteen months, dying at Chicopee MA in January 1828.

narrative_blocks.header: Three states across forty years of marriage
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel and Hannah's life together threaded across three states: their first three children were born in Northampton MA (1788-1791); the next six in Chester MA (1793-1802); and they finally returned to Samuel's native Windsor CT where he died in 1826. The pattern reflects the era's flexible Connecticut-Massachusetts borderland economy.

narrative_blocks.header: Northampton silversmith — surviving spoons in Deerfield
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Stiles opened a clock and watch shop on Shop Row in Northampton in 1785 — a few rods south of the Court-House — and worked as a clockmaker, watchmaker, jeweler, and silversmith for six years before partnering with Jedidiah Baldwin in July 1791. Two silver spoons bearing his 'SS' maker's mark survive in the collection of Historic Deerfield. The partnership dissolved within a year and Samuel left Northampton for Chester MA in 1792.

career.organization: Samuel Stiles shop, Shop Row, Northampton MA
career.notes: Opened shop in Northampton 1785, a few rods south of the Court-House. Advertised in Springfield Hampshire Herald July 27, 1785. Made silver including spoons with maker's mark 'SS' (two examples in Historic Deerfield collection). Took apprentices Joel Brown and Horace Clark, c.1789-1793.

Partnership with Jedidiah Baldwin (1769-1849), formed July 20, 1791 in shop nearly opposite the Meeting-house in Northampton — clock and watch business, jewelry, all branches of goldsmith's business. Baldwin had served apprenticeship to Thomas Harland of Norwich CT. Partnership lasted approximately one year before Samuel left Northampton.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909), entry 461
Henry Reed Stiles, The Stiles Family in America (1895), Samuel Stiles entry 233

He was 50 years old at the time — old for a militiaman — but the Connecticut coast was being threatened by British naval blockade, and middle-aged men were drawn into local defense.

The pattern reflects the era's flexible Connecticut-Massachusetts borderland economy.

parents.father_research_notes: Of Windsor CT. Not in JSON.
parents.mother_research_notes: Of Windsor CT. Maiden name not documented in EH 461. Not in JSON.

narrative_blocks.header: Windsor Ellsworth family — Chief Justice was kin
narrative_blocks.body: She was a daughter of Jonathan Ellsworth of Windsor Connecticut, of the same Windsor Ellsworth family that produced Oliver Ellsworth (1745-1807) — third Chief Justice of the United States and co-author of the Connecticut Compromise. Specific kinship between Hannah's father and Oliver has not yet been documented but the Windsor Ellsworth naming patterns place them in the same kin network.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at Chicopee Massachusetts after husband Samuel
narrative_blocks.body: She survived her husband Samuel Stiles by fifteen months — Samuel died at Chicopee Massachusetts in October 1826, Hannah followed there on 12 January 1828. The Connecticut-born couple's migration to Chicopee tracked their daughter Delia, who lived there.

narrative_blocks.header: Longest-lived of nine, a widow for decades
narrative_blocks.body: Delia Stiles Van Horn outlived all eight of her Stiles siblings and survived her husband Ruel, a Chicopee farmer, by twenty-four years. She lived to eighty-two.

narrative_blocks.header: Her son was Lincoln’s consul at Marseilles
narrative_blocks.body: Her son George W. Van Horn was appointed United States Consul at Marseilles by President Lincoln and served through 1866. He later founded a newspaper in Iowa and wrote novels.

career.notes: Descendant of Born Van Horn of Saxe-Coburg, Germany (per Stiles book extended descent of Delia).

naming_inspiration.description: Named for his uncle Ellsworth Stiles (HD4142, 1797-1823, died age 26 unmarried) — three years after his uncle's death. Two Ellsworth Stiles namesakes across this Van Horne family: HD4152 (this entry, born 1826) and HD4150 (Ellsworth Stiles Van Horne, born 1874).

narrative_blocks.header: Died at eight, third of Delia's four
narrative_blocks.body: Benjamin R Van Horn was born October 6, 1830 in Chicopee MA and died August 13, 1839 at age 8. He was the third child of Delia Stiles Van Horn and Reuel Van Horn, and the only one of their four children not to reach adulthood. His brother George W Van Horne, born three years after Benjamin's death, would never know him.

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of George W. Van Horne, Muscatine editor and former US Consul at Marseilles.

burial.plot_notes: Greenwood Cemetery, Muscatine IA — with wife Mary Iowa Morrow and daughter Lulu Magoon.

notable.notable_blurb: Lincoln-appointed US Consul at Marseilles; Muscatine Tribune founder

education.notes: High school and academic course (specific school not documented).

education.school_name: Law office of Hon. Charles R. Ladd
education.notes: Read law with Hon. Charles R. Ladd, then and for many years Auditor of State (Massachusetts).

education.school_name: Law office of Hon. E. B. Gillette
education.notes: Westfield, MA. Continued law studies with Hon. E. B. Gillette of Westfield MA.
Bar admission, Iowa. Completed law studies under the Iowa Code at Cloud & O'Connor, Muscatine. Admitted to the bar 1856.

career.notes: Partnership with Hon. D.C. Cloud, then Attorney General of Iowa.
Stumped for John C. Fremont (1856) and Abraham Lincoln (1860). Chairman of Republican County Committee in Muscatine County 1856-1860.

Appointed during the first week of President Lincoln's administration in March 1861. Removed by President Andrew Johnson in 1866 amid Reconstruction tensions. While in office, attempted to distribute pamphlets encouraging French emigration to the United States under the 1864 Act to Encourage Immigration — corresponded with Minister John Bigelow about the French government's refusal to authorize distribution (documented in 1866 Foreign Relations papers).
Accepted invitation from the Arkansas Republican State Central Committee to edit the new state organ at Little Rock. Soon resigned amid the unsettled political conditions.

Appointed under the Reconstruction Acts to register voters in Scott County AR. His Reconstruction experience reportedly turned him against Black political participation in the South — a political evolution from Lincoln Republican to Reconstruction critic that he carried into his later editorial work.

Founded Muscatine Tribune (daily and weekly) on returning from Massachusetts winter 1870. Purchased Muscatine Courier and merged under Tribune name. Betts Brothers admitted partners for several years. Severed relations with the Tribune and the Muscatine Journal in 1887.

career.organization: Muscatine News Company (Muscatine Daily and Weekly News)
career.notes: Muscatine News Company incorporated December 1887. Served as Secretary of the company and Editor-in-Chief until his death.
Federal postmaster appointment during Cleveland's second term, despite Van Horne's drift from Republican orthodoxy.

Published a novel 'Tom Rockley' through the American Publishing Company of Hartford — the same Hartford publisher who issued Mark Twain's books. Also wrote serial pieces 'The Old Settlers' Chair' (Muscatine pioneer history), 'Storied Scenes of Europe,' 'Men and Women I Have Seen,' 'Farmer Whiting Letters,' and 'Anonymous Portraits of Muscatine Women.'

narrative_blocks.header: Lincoln consul at Marseilles, first week appointment
narrative_blocks.body: George W Van Horne was appointed U.S. Consul at Marseilles, France during the first week of Abraham Lincoln's administration in March 1861, having served on the stump in Iowa for both the Fremont 1856 and Lincoln 1860 campaigns. He left immediately for his post with his family, served until President Johnson removed him in 1866, and during his tenure attempted (unsuccessfully) to distribute French-language pamphlets encouraging emigration to the United States.

narrative_blocks.header: Arkansas Reconstruction Registrar, then Iowa journalist
narrative_blocks.body: After Johnson's 1866 removal, Van Horne briefly edited the Arkansas Republican state organ at Little Rock before becoming a Reconstruction Registrar for Scott County. His Arkansas experience reportedly turned him against the Reconstruction program he had been appointed to administer. Returning to Iowa in 1870, he founded the Muscatine Tribune (purchasing the Muscatine Courier and merging them) and edited Muscatine newspapers until his death in 1895.

narrative_blocks.header: Published novel through Mark Twain's Hartford publisher
narrative_blocks.body: Van Horne wrote a novel titled 'Tom Rockley' that was published by the American Publishing Company of Hartford — the same firm that issued Mark Twain's books. He also wrote serial pieces including 'The Old Settlers' Chair' (Muscatine pioneer history), 'Storied Scenes of Europe,' and 'Anonymous Portraits of Muscatine Women' — making him both a Muscatine civic figure and a published literary author.

narrative_blocks.header: Married first woman he saw landing at Muscatine
narrative_blocks.body: George W Van Horne arrived in Muscatine in May 1855 from Massachusetts; Mary Iowa Morrow was the first woman he saw on landing. They married September 15, 1858, when she was 15 — she was the only daughter of Dr. James G Morrow (one of Muscatine's founders) and stepdaughter at the time of George's law partner D.C. Cloud. She is claimed in family tradition as 'the first native-born bride of Iowa.'

sources.label: History of Muscatine County Iowa (1888) — George W. Van Horne entry
1866 Foreign Relations of the United States (39th Congress, 2nd Session, Pt I)

narrative_blocks.header: Drowned in Lake Erie at twenty-five, June 1818
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Stiles drowned in Lake Erie on June 24, 1818, age 25 — only eight years before his father, his namesake, would die at Windsor. Lake Erie in 1818 was the gateway to the Connecticut Western Reserve where his second cousin Leonard Tuttle would migrate eighteen years later; whether young Samuel was traveling to the Reserve when he drowned is undocumented but historically plausible.

cross_connections.display_label: Second cousins via Esther Hooker, both migrated to Ohio
First cousins, sons of brothers Job and Samuel Stiles

narrative_blocks.header: Migrated to Ohio, like his second cousin Leonard
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Stiles was born in Chester MA in 1795 and died in Ohio — joining the same Connecticut Western Reserve migration that brought his second cousin Leonard Tuttle to Medina County by 1836. Both descend from Esther Hooker Stiles, the gen-3 ancestor whose family produced two parallel mid-1800s Ohio settlements.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Phelps' carries his paternal grandmother Hannah Stiles Stiles's mother's family — Mary Phelps married Lt. Samuel Stiles, and her surname descended to her great-grandson as a middle name marker.

narrative_blocks.header: Married a year, no issue, both spouses dead
narrative_blocks.body: Amelia Stiles married William Welch on August 14, 1821 in Massachusetts. He died in 1822; she followed on December 30, 1822 at age 20. They left no children.

narrative_blocks.header: William died young
narrative_blocks.body: William died young in 1822.

narrative_blocks.header: Twin who survived, two marriages, one son
narrative_blocks.body: Julia Stiles was born June 10, 1802 in Chester MA — the same day as her twin Amelia, who would die at twenty. Julia married Calvin Cooley in 1823, the year after Amelia's death, then remarried Sidney Crawford in 1856; one son is documented from the second marriage. Death date not in EH 461.

military_service.notes: Per The Stiles Family in America (BIB008) entry #230: served in the Revolution and was at Horse Neck from May 1781 to March 1782. Horse Neck (Greenwich CT) was an active Revolutionary War outpost under occasional British raid throughout the war.
Served in the Revolution at Horse Neck (now Greenwich CT, on the western frontier facing British-held New York), from May 1781 to March 1782.

narrative_blocks.header: Inherited mansion, lost all endorsing for friend
narrative_blocks.body: Ashbel Stiles inherited the family mansion and a very comfortable property from his father in North Haven Connecticut. But in an evil hour he endorsed for a friend and lost his all. Soon after he removed to Windsor Connecticut, and thence to Norwich Massachusetts — now Huntington — near his daughter Ruth Stiles Ellis.

narrative_blocks.header: Hannah died Norwich one month before Ashbel did
narrative_blocks.body: After fifty-one years of marriage, Ashbel and his cousin-wife Hannah Stiles both died at Norwich (now Huntington) Massachusetts in autumn 1810 — both at age seventy-five. Hannah went first in September; Ashbel followed in October, approximately one month later.

narrative_blocks.header: Son thrown from a horse, killed in Jamaica
narrative_blocks.body: His son Benjamin Stiles died at Jamaica in the West Indies from injuries sustained being thrown from a horse. The Stiles Family in America entry 230 records the death without further circumstance — a son lost an ocean from home to a single fall.

narrative_blocks.header: Horse Neck garrison 1781 after financial ruin
narrative_blocks.body: Forty-six at enlistment, he came to Revolutionary militia service well after most of his cohort. Financial ruin from endorsing for a friend had already emptied the family mansion he had inherited from his father.

parents.father_research_notes: Lt. Samuel Stiles of Windsor CT

bio.notes: Hannah Stiles married her cousin Ashbell Stiles. She was daughter of Lt. Samuel Stiles and Mary Phelps of Windsor CT. Same surname before and after marriage.

I00126.gravestone_inscription: This monument is erected to the memory of the Rev. Isaac Stiles, A.M., who was born in Windsor, July 30th, 1697, received a liberal education at Yale College; ordained to the pastoral office in the church of North Haven, November 11th, 1724, where he served in the ministry 36 years, and died May 14th, 1760, aged 63. ...he preached the gospel with fervor and fidelity. A friend to pure and undefiled religion, with a charitable benevolence to all mankind. Mors Mihi vita Est.

cross_connections.display_label: was father of his first wife, Keziah

narrative_blocks.header: Thirty-six years in North Haven’s pulpit
narrative_blocks.body: Yale 1722, Stiles held the North Haven pastorate for thirty-six years and raised — by his first wife — Ezra Stiles, the Enlightenment polymath who became Yale’s seventh president. His Hooker-descended children came by his second wife, Esther Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: He preached against his own melon thief
narrative_blocks.body: Spotting a parishioner stealing his melons during the Sunday intermission, Stiles named him from the afternoon pulpit: “I saw you, John Johnson, thou son of Belial… enter my garden and steal my melons.” His son Ezra called it “rather close preaching, but characteristic of the times.”

narrative_blocks.header: His first wife was the poet’s daughter
narrative_blocks.body: Keziah Taylor, daughter of the metaphysical poet Rev. Edward Taylor of Westfield, died in childbed in December 1727 — twenty-five years old, two years married. Within the year Stiles remarried, to Esther Hooker of Farmington, who bore his ten North Haven children.

narrative_blocks.header: First of his blood liberally educated
narrative_blocks.body: Yale 1722 — “the first of the name and blood that had a liberal education in America” — he published “A Looking-glass for Changlings” against political turncoats in 1743. His son Ezra became president of the college; his epitaph fills a monument at North Haven.

cross_connections.display_label: Esther Hooker Stiles was the great-grandmother of Horace Stiles who.
Esther Hooker Stiles was the great-grandmother of Horace Stiles who.

narrative_blocks.header: Second wife of Rev. Isaac Stiles
narrative_blocks.body: She married Rev. Isaac Stiles of North Haven as his second wife. His first wife Keziah Taylor — daughter of the great Puritan poet Rev. Edward Taylor — had died in childbirth on December 4, 1727.

narrative_blocks.header: Raised Ezra Stiles, future Yale president
narrative_blocks.body: She raised her stepson Ezra Stiles from infancy after his birth mother Keziah Taylor's death. He became President of Yale (1778-1795) and one of the most important intellectuals of the American Founding.

narrative_blocks.header: Ten children with Stiles between 1729 and 1750
narrative_blocks.body: She and Isaac Stiles had ten children of their own between 1729 and 1750. Only Ezra was Keziah's son from the first marriage; all others were Esther's, carrying the Stiles-Hooker line forward through North Haven.

narrative_blocks.header: Her name traveled five generations as Esther Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: The name 'Esther Hooker' became a heritable token across five generations of Stiles women — from her (1702) to her granddaughter (1760), and onward through Alice Stiles Mills (1845). Her name carried farther than her lineage.
